ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper combines two existing ideas—eligibility traces (Sec. 2.1 and Q(λ) discussion) and deep recurrent Q-networks (DRQN, Sec. 2.2.1)—and implements a practical forward-view trace mechanism on sub-trajectories to make traces computationally feasible (Sec. 3). It provides empirical evidence in Atari (Pong and Tennis, Sec. 4) and highlights optimizer effects (RMSprop vs Adam), which is a useful empirical/engineering contribution but not a fundamentally new method. Given prior mentions that traces in deep RL are limited (van Seijen & Sutton (2014), Hausknecht & Stone (2015)), the work is a moderate, incremental advance rather than a highly novel theoretical innovation.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well-organized (intro → background → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its goals early, with notation for MDPs, n-step returns and Q(λ) introduced consistently. However, presentation suffers from missing/unclear figures and tables (Figures 2/3 and Table 1 are referenced but not shown here), numerous duplicated/garbled paragraphs near the end, and some typos/phrasing issues (e.g. “differnet”, “an of 0.05”, awkward algorithm formatting) that reduce readability. Overall the exposition is adequate but several sections need cleanup and proofreading to be fully clear.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main empirical claims (eligibility traces + DRQN improve learning and optimizer choice matters) are supported by the presented Pong and Tennis experiments (Sec. 4.1–4.2) and the algorithm description (Alg. 1), and the mathematical exposition of traces and DQN/DRQN is standard and appears correct. However, the experimental methodology is weak: only two games, no reported random seeds or variance/statistical tests, small batch size and few baselines (no direct comparisons to Double DQN/prioritized replay) so results may not generalize and optimizer effects may be confounded by target-network/update-frequency choices (acknowledged in Discussion). Overall the technical approach is plausible but evidence is limited and some claims are insufficiently supported.
